coredata.dc:title: Upcycling biowaste into advanced carbon materials via low-temperature plasma hybrid system: applications, mechanisms, strategies and future prospects

coredata.dc:description: This review focuses on the recent advances in the sustainable conversion of biowaste to valuable carbonaceous materials. This study summarizes the significant progress in biowaste-derived carbon materials (BCMs) via a plasma hybrid system. This includes systematic studies like AI-based multi-coupling systems, promising synthesis strategies from an economic point of view, and their potential applications towards energy, environment, and biomedicine. Plasma modified BCM has a new transition lattice phase and exhibits high resilience, while fabrication and formation mechanisms of BCMs are reviewed in plasma hybrid system. A unique 2D structure can be designed and formulated from the biowaste with fascinating physicochemical properties like high surface area, unique defect sites, and excellent conductivity. The structure of BCMs offers various activated sites for element doping and it shows satisfactory adsorption capability, and dynamic performance in the field of electrochemistry. In recent years, many studies have been reported on the biowaste conversion into valuable materials for various applications. Synthesis methods are an indispensable factor that directly affects the structure and properties of BCMs. Therefore, it is imperative to review the facile synthesis methods and the mechanisms behind the formation of BCMs derived from the low-temperature plasma hybrid system, which is the necessity to obtain BCMs having desirable structure and properties by choosing a suitable synthesis process. Advanced carbon–neutral materials could be widely synthesized as catalysts for application in environmental remediation, energy conversion and storage, and biotechnology.

head.ce:title: Upcycling biowaste into advanced carbon materials via low-temperature plasma hybrid system: applications, mechanisms, strategies and future prospects

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This review focuses on the recent advances in the sustainable conversion of biowaste to valuable carbonaceous materials. This study summarizes the significant progress in biowaste-derived carbon materials (BCMs) via a plasma hybrid system. This includes systematic studies like AI-based multi-coupling systems, promising synthesis strategies from an economic point of view, and their potential applications towards energy, environment, and biomedicine. Plasma modified BCM has a new transition lattice phase and exhibits high resilience, while fabrication and formation mechanisms of BCMs are reviewed in plasma hybrid system. A unique 2D structure can be designed and formulated from the biowaste with fascinating physicochemical properties like high surface area, unique defect sites, and excellent conductivity. The structure of BCMs offers various activated sites for element doping and it shows satisfactory adsorption capability, and dynamic performance in the field of electrochemistry. In recent years, many studies have been reported on the biowaste conversion into valuable materials for various applications. Synthesis methods are an indispensable factor that directly affects the structure and properties of BCMs. Therefore, it is imperative to review the facile synthesis methods and the mechanisms behind the formation of BCMs derived from the low-temperature plasma hybrid system, which is the necessity to obtain BCMs having desirable structure and properties by choosing a suitable synthesis process. Advanced carbon–neutral materials could be widely synthesized as catalysts for application in environmental remediation, energy conversion and storage, and biotechnology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of highly available biowaste as renewable raw material is a promising approach to produce carbon-containing materials for environmental health and remediation. This review focuses on the current status of value-added carbon materials derived from biowaste via plasma hybrid systems. Unconventional carbon–neutral materials derived from biowaste conversion can sequester carbon into ecosystems and remove pollutants, thereby improving environmental remediation. Hence, we provide fundamental information on advanced carbon–neutral materials derived from the biowaste, which includes the effect of biowaste components on the fabrication process, structural properties, and application of materials. Furthermore, we have discussed advanced carbon–neutral materials used as a catalyst in energy conversion and storage, environmental health, and remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of biowaste on climate, environment, and ecosystem
ce:section.ce:para: Biowaste accounts for a large proportion of the total solid waste generated globally, with the total amount expected to reach 100 billion tons per year (Pitakaso et al., 2024). As demonstrated in Fig. 1 , biowaste basically consists of sewage sludge, municipal solid biowaste, crop waste, and livestock manure (Oviedo-Ocaña et al., 2023; Prado-Acebo et al., 2024). Sewage sludge mainly contains high concentrations of heavy metals, organic pollutants, pathogens, and other harmful substances, thus easily generates malodors, contaminates water bodies, as well as spreads many diseases through the food chain and groundwater (Esperschuetz et al., 2016). Municipal solid biowaste includes food waste from households and restaurants and also, biodegradable waste from gardens (Ardolino et al., 2020). Municipal solid biowaste has a high content of moisture, organic material, and mineral salts. A large amount of leachate (mainly oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing organic pollutants) is generated in the direct landfill which eventually leads to soil and groundwater contamination, as well as generates greenhouse gases (Watson et al., 2018a).

Crop wastes include the residues of straws, cereals, fruits, and vegetables. The high amount of CO, CO2, SO2, NOx, and soot, is generated by the incineration of the crop wastes which seriously damage the atmospheric environment (Jena & Singh, 2022). Livestock manure not only has high chemical oxygen demand, but also contains some nitrogen, phosphorus, and other elements, leading to the deterioration of surface water quality and eutrophication (Wang et al., 2019a). In addition, livestock manure also has hazardous substances, such as ammonia, amines, sulfides, urates, and pathogenic bacteria, which ultimately elevates air pollution. Therefore, the development and improvement in the treatment of biowaste resulted in a variety of value-added products and other forms of energy which are of great significance for the protection of the environment and resources, and the growth of the economy worldwide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of biowaste components on carbon materials properties
ce:section.ce:para: Biowaste contains various elements as presented in chemical composition, as shown in Fig. 2 . Besides, it is a renewable raw material that can be majorly used for the preparation of carbonaceous materials. The main elements present in the biowaste are C, H, O, N, S, etc., in which the C and O elements account for more than 90 % of the total biowaste. Normally, the carbonaceous material is mainly composed of C element in its structure which is obtained by the anoxic pyrolysis of biowaste, and it also contains small amounts of O, H, N, S, ash, and inorganic minerals (Wang et al., 2018a; Zhao et al., 2013). These heteroatoms can present as in-situ doping sites in the structure of carbon material. And the material can be tuned with adjustable physical and chemical properties depending on the type of elements present in its structure (Wang et al., 2016). There are alkaline carbon materials naturally derived from some biowastes which contain mineral salt sites in their structure. These carbon materials contain abundant minerals (mainly oxides or hydroxides such as Ca, K, Mg, Na, and Si), which are highly important in electrochemical energy conversion and storage (Kang et al., 2015).

The presence of sulfur-containing functional groups in the carbon material can increase the surface negative charge, thereby improving the electrostatic attraction between the adjacent structure. Pyridine nitrogen is the main nitrogen-containing functional group present in the carbon material, which mainly comes from the deamination or dehydrogenation chemical process of protein and amino acid present in the biowaste. Also, the pyridine nitrogen is considered as an electroactive functional group (Lyu et al., 2020). In addition, as the carbonization temperature of the biowaste increases, the degree of aromatic ring condensation in the carbon material enhances thereby increasing the production of fused-ring compounds. This enables carbon materials to exhibit good chemical stability, heat resistance, thermal and electrical properties (Fan et al., 2016). Solid biowaste has rich forms and complex structures. Among them, the three-dimensional structure is relatively stable. The original basic structure can still be retained after removing the parts decomposed during the carbonization process. For example, stone crab shells have a multi-layer stacked nanostructure after carbonization (Yao et al., 2013). And, carrots have a connected three-dimensional porous structure after carbonization (Liu et al., 2020a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced carbon–neutral materials for energy and environmental science
ce:section.ce:para: Solid biowaste can be converted into porous carbon materials with high specific surface area, diverse porous structures, high electron density, and other properties through physical activation, chemical activation, template method, hydrothermal carbonization, and other processes (Singh et al., 2019). These materials can be widely used in carbon dioxide capture, water purification, catalysis, hydrogen storage, and energy storage, providing effective solutions to energy and environmental issues (Lu & Zhao, 2017). The presence of hydroxyl functional groups, microporous structures, and heteroatoms like N and O are the main factors in controlling the adsorption of CO2 in carbon materials. Activated carbon materials derived from agro-waste biomass have been used in gold recovery from waste streams with an uptake capability of 1679.74 ± 37.66 mg/g(Bediako et al., 2024). N-doped porous carbons were produced from waste cork via urea-assisted hydrothermal approach to capture volatile organic compounds (He et al., 2024). The results showed that pore sizes (1.71–2.28 nm) play an important role in mitigating volatile organic compounds (VOC). And the VOC capture efficiency reached 867.03 mg/g owing to the high specific surface area (1864.89 m2/g) and excellent pore structure of these N-doped porous carbons.

In water purification applications, the adsorption of pollutants in wastewater using carbon materials mainly depends on the porous structure, specific surface area, surface interface, and oxygen-containing functional groups of the carbon material surface. Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2020b) prepared three kinds of multi-stage porous carbon materials by the carbonization of biowaste at 800 ℃ and KOH activation. The carbon material prepared from hardwood alkali lignin had a specific surface area of 2852 m2/g, a maximum oxygen content of 10.6 %, and a maximum adsorption capacity for methylene blue of 1671.7 mg/g. Carbon materials are used as catalysts or catalyst support because of their high chemical stability, heat resistance, and electrical conductivity. Asgharzadeh et al. (Asgharzadeh et al., 2021) have used modified magnetic biochar as a heterogeneous catalyst to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation activity and antibacterial study of tetracycline, the results showed that the mineralization rate of tetracycline within 45 min was 80.24 %, and no growth inhibition zone was formed after the purification process of tetracycline, and the effect of antibiotics was significantly reduced or eliminated. Pan et al. (Pan et al., 2014) prepared nitrogen-doped porous carbon nanosheets by treating Ginkgo leaves at high temperatures. The obtained carbon material exhibits better redox reaction activity in an alkaline medium. Researchers have studied the recovery of the carbon materials from wastewater (Sghiouri idrissi et al., 2024). The spent carbon materials can be reused after pretreatment via multiple approaches like thermal, physical and chemical methods. The activating agent of H3PO4 presented the highest performance with a 53 % increase of the specific surface area compared to the spent carbon materials.

Carbon materials are promising materials for hydrogen storage, lithium batteries, and supercapacitors because of their diverse microstructures, tunable physicochemical properties, and abundant heteroatoms present in their structure. Yuan et al. (Yuan et al., 2019a) reported the porous carbon material synthesized from peanut dreg and used it as a heterogeneous catalyst in the field of supercapacitors. The catalytic study shows that when the current density was 0.5 A•g−1, the hydrogen absorption efficiency and specific capacitance reached 3.03 % and 449F•g−1, respectively. Sun et al. (Liu et al., 2019) focused on the preparations of Litchi-shell-based porous carbon material used as cathode material in lithium-sulfur battery applications. The first discharge-specific capacity reached 1667 mAh•g−1 and remained at 612 mAh•g−1 after 200 cycles, proving that the catalyst showed good cycle stability. In recent years, the application of biowaste as a supercapacitor is shown in Table 1 .

Biowaste not only consumes a lot of space, but also pollutes the planet soil, groundwater, and air when directly landfilled and incinerated. In addition to this, toxic compounds present in biowaste may pose a threat to human health throughout the food chain. Due to its elemental composition and microstructure, biowaste can be used as a precursor for the preparation of functional carbon materials. Different types of biowaste have different elemental compositions and microstructures. Biowaste-derived carbon materials exhibit excellent properties and have achieved great success in various applications such as carbon dioxide capture, water purification, catalysis, hydrogen, and energy storage.

The structure, properties, and application of carbon-based materials have been reviewed in recent frontier studies. A structural representation of BCM is organized to showcase the unique 2D structure of BCM in Fig. 3 . Two simple methods have been developed for the preparation of BCM, including top-down and bottom-up approaches. These methods have shortcomings such as high energy consumption, long preparation time, precision equipment requirements, and low carbon material yield. Therefore, these traditional technologies need to be modified to improve the production of BCM so that carbon materials can be used in more applications. Advanced low-temperature plasma technology is introduced for the fabrication of BCM. Recent studies on the synthesis of BCM using carbonization, activation, heteroatom doping, and template techniques are summarized. This review summarizes the fabrication and formation mechanisms of BCMs in low-temperature plasma hybrid systems. BCM, a relatively cutting-edge discipline, relies on understanding its formation mechanism and better-manipulating manufacturing technologies and applications in various fields to maximize its impact.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the BCMs production via low-temperature plasma hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of BCM formation by plasma technology needs to be studied to better control various experimental conditions in the plasma process. In particular, the cutting-edge scientific issues of BCMs rely more on the understanding of the formation mechanism of BCMs, which is conducive to better controlling the synthesis, performance, and final application of BCMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic concept of low-temperature plasma hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: The ions present in low-temperature plasma technology remain in a non-equilibrium state, where the electron temperature is unbalanced (Lee & Kim, 2018b). It contains the ionized and excited states of atoms, molecules, and free radicals. It does not usually exist in thermal equilibrium at ambient temperature and therefore has reactive properties only seen in combustion systems and thermal discharges at higher temperatures (>727 ℃) (Coutinho et al., 2018; Duan et al., 2017). The highly reactive particles in low-temperature plasma technology are also highly chemically active. Therefore, chemical reactions can occur in non-thermal plasmas even at lower temperatures. Plasma technology has the advantages of fast processing speed, streamlined equipment, and easy implementation of reactions. Therefore, it can be widely used in the degradation of organic pollutants, as well as the preparation and modification of carbon-based materials, catalyst synthesis, etc. (Chang et al., 2020b; Liu et al., 2020c; Wang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2018b). For instance, Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2024) constructed a novel Silicon/graphene composites by transition lattice interfaces constructed using plasma, and showed that the modified material can have a new transition lattice phase interface with a larger lattice spacing, and exhibits high resilience during the charge and discharge cycle (irreversible volume expansion during the first charge and discharge is only 33.1 %). However, traditional low-temperature plasma technologies have some drawbacks, such as low energy efficiency and the formation of toxic by-products during the reaction process (Chang et al., 2020b). To overcome the drawbacks of low-temperature plasma, researchers try to integrate plasma with other existing technologies and approaches to form a low-temperature plasma hybrid system. The techniques under low-temperature plasma hybrid systems have been classified in Table 2 . So far, studies have been conducted on various factors affecting the implementation of low-temperature plasma technology, to achieve the goal of high efficiency, growth in the economy, and no release of pollution, to meet the basic requirements of different industrial applications and to develop the sustainable strategy (Chang et al., 2020b). Low-temperature plasma hybrid system refers to the application of combining low-temperature plasma technology with other related technologies, which can exert the residual effects of two or more technologies and achieve efficient degradation of organic pollutants and a high yield of BCM.

For example, plasma catalysis technology refers to the non-uniform combination of plasma and catalytic materials. The mixing of these two processes can produce results in a more efficient approach that is sometimes not achievable by either plasma or catalysis respectively (Whitehead, 2016). Lee et al. (Lee & Kim, 2018b) found that Mn2O3-coated glass beads combined with low-temperature plasma technology can achieve a maximum methanol yield of 12 % at temperatures below 100 °C, which represents a potential and efficient method to directly produce methane from methane through a low-temperature plasma and catalyst mixing system.

Ohshima et al. (Ohshima et al., 2007) used fibrous activated-carbon textiles as adsorbents for the degradation of gaseous acetaldehyde and dielectric barrier discharge electrodes. The results show that when an acetaldehyde input concentration of 200 ppm is used, the acetaldehyde output concentrations observed at applied voltages of 13, 10, and 5 kV are 30, 45, and 120 ppm, respectively. The plasma instability derived from 5 kV voltage resulted in significantly lower energy efficiency than the voltage at 10 and 13 kV. Low-temperature plasma can also assist biological treatment and make up for the shortcomings of biological treatment technology (Chang et al., 2020b). In the early stage, volatile organic compounds are pretreated to reduce the density of volatile organic compounds and increase the concentration of water-soluble pollutants, so that problematic difficult-to-biodegrade volatile organic compounds can be easily converted into more biodegradable intermediates.

Volatile organic compounds (VOCs), emitted from industry and agriculture, vehicle exhaust and fossil fuel combustion, have caused serious harm to human health and the environment. The existing VOCs common processing methods are shown in the following Table 3 . The collaborative degradation of VOCs by plasma and biological treatment technology can not only solve the challenges of biological treatment technology, but also solve the problems of incomplete degradation and secondary pollution caused by plasma technology. Hybrid design has complementary advantages and has become one of the most promising VOC degradation technologies (Chang et al., 2020b). In the field of low-temperature plasma technology, the preparation of carbon-based materials like graphene has also been achieved. It is a breakthrough in the synthesis of graphene-like materials through the preparation of graphene film by low-temperature plasma-assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD) activation and decomposition of carbon source (Kim et al., 2019).

The main disadvantage of traditional thermal CVD synthesis technology is that the technology is limited by high temperatures (generally around 1000 °C), which not only limits the large-scale production of graphene films but also increases production costs. It is vital to combine plasma with the CVD process to form a plasma-enhanced CVD technology to overcome the disadvantages of the thermal CVD process. The integrated system can reduce the growth temperature of graphene to 400–600 ℃, and achieve the production of graphene at an industrial level which increases the possibilities of the various applications of graphene (Akinwande et al., 2019).

Compared with traditional thermal CVD, plasma-enhanced CVD can greatly reduce the synthesis temperature and accelerate the growth rate of graphene (Kim et al., 2019). In particular, the planar-type surface wave plasma at low gas pressure (<200 mTorr) can achieve a continuous synthesis of large-scale graphene films (Kim et al., 2019). However, the interaction between low-temperature plasma technology and other technologies is complex and changeable. There are still many unclear paths that are still pending to study. This section aims to summarize the recent studies of low-temperature plasma mixing systems in the preparation of biomass material and to provide some theoretical guidance for the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of BCMs via plasma-based and plasma-assisted techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Biomass is a renewable energy source that contains large amounts of organic carbon in its structure. The BCMs and other valuable chemicals can be obtained by different methods, such as liquefaction, gasification, pyrolysis, and microbial engineering. To obtain an eco-friendly method for the preparation or modification of carbon-based materials, plasma technology can be combined with pyrolysis, gasification, catalysis, and other technologies to produce carbon-based materials suitable for various industrial applications. It has broad application prospects in environmental remediation, materials science, energy storage, and other fields (Wang et al., 2021a).

The BCMs with ideal and ordered surface properties could be synthesized through careful manipulation of the fundamental properties of the plasma, including many different plasma generation methods, power input, frequency control, as well as the precise control of selected reaction gases by 3D printed geometries (Sun et al., 2020). Blanquet et al. (Blanquet et al., 2019) used a non-thermal plasma system to convert biomass pyrolysis gas into hydrogen-rich gas, proving that plasma treatment of biomass pyrolysis gas can significantly increase the total gas production and triple the hydrogen production. Niu et al. (Niu et al., 2017) have reported the adsorbents with a high removal efficiency of mercury (Hg0) from the corn straw by pyrolysis-plasma-assisted modification. Plasma modification technology can significantly change the content and type of surface functional groups that exist uniformly on the surface of biochar with little damage to the pore structure of biochar. The content of oxygen-containing functional groups on the biochar surface can be increased to accelerate its adsorption of Hg0 when sufficient oxygen content is present on the biochar surface under appropriate modification time conditions.

So far, plasma technology has been widely studied for the surface modification of carbon materials (Table 4 ). The plasmas of different gas types, such as oxygen, nitrogen-containing gases (N2 and NH3 account for the main part), air, and other gases (such as methane or ethanol), can introduce a variety of functional groups on the surface of carbon materials. Etching or cross-linking can improve surface roughness, change the chemical composition, and improve the surface properties of carbon materials. Compared with other surface modification methods, low-temperature plasma technology has a higher utilization rate of modified samples, reduces the overall carbon loss and secondary pollution caused by traditional methods, and has broad application prospects (Ahmed et al., 2022).

Almost all solid carbonaceous materials can be treated by low-temperature plasma without causing damage to the material (Shah et al., 2019; Xz et al.). The modified BCMs can be applied to many fields such as electrochemical electrodes, supercapacitors, catalysts, biosensors, hydrogen storage, etc. Modified BCMs have also been reported as excellent adsorbents, antibacterial agents, decolorization, and deodorization agents, and are widely used in the prevention and control of air pollution and water deterioration (Sun et al., 2020). Yi et al. (Yi et al., 2019) found that the specific surface area of biochar could be enhanced from 3.8 m2/g to 275.3 m2/g by plasma activation technique for the removal of uranyl from wastewater, and the removal rate remained at nearly 90 % after 5 cycles. The adsorption efficiency of uranyl with the recovered biochar has been increased to 90 % under the conditions of pH 5 and temperature of 25 ℃. Zhou et al. (Zhou et al., 2021) enhanced the surface functionality and catalytic performance of biochar with low-temperature plasma technology. Through surface functionalization or modification of biochar, more oxygen-containing functional groups are introduced, thereby improving the physical and chemical properties of biochar, ultimately leading to higher catalytic activity. After plasma treatment, the adsorption rate of biochar for metals increased significantly, and the adsorption capacity for Pb2+ increased by 40 %. Under the combined action of plasma and potassium hydroxide, the pore volume and specific surface area of activated carbon are significantly increased, and the performance of capacitive electrodes is improved by 30 %.

Zhang et al. (Xz et al.) introduced a large number of N-containing groups into biochar by ammonia plasma treatment technology, which promoted the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants with the help of biochar. The π-π stacking between the delocalized system and the aromatic structure of ciprofloxacin enhanced the interaction between ciprofloxacin and the biochar surface. The adsorption capacity and maximum removal rate of ciprofloxacin by treated biochar were 2.61 to 4.26 times and 2.25 to 2.71 times higher than that of original biochar, respectively. Hu et al. (Hu et al., 2020) changed the plasma activation carrier gas and replaced the carbon in the five-ring and six-ring carbon structures under high pressure to obtain nitrogen atoms, thereby generating active sites that are conducive to the adsorption of heavy metals (Pb, Cu, and Cd (II)). It shows that plasma activation conditions (pressure, temperature, and carrier gas) have a positive impact on the study of biochar activation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the BCMs production via low-temperature plasma hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the low-temperature plasma mixing system in BCM production involves the action of high-energy ions such as electrons, charged ions, electrically neutral molecules, and free radicals on the surface of carbon materials. Processes such as etching, cross-linking, and introduction of functional groups on the material surface lead to changes in the surface properties of carbon materials. Low-temperature plasma technology can convert energy directly into chemical bonds of reactant molecules through electric discharge, thereby reducing the energy required for conventional reactions. Combined with the catalyst, it can form a plasma sheath on the catalyst surface, reduce catalyst coking, and increase the adsorption and desorption of reactants and products on the catalyst (Ye et al., 2019).

The selection of catalysts plays an important role in biofuel production using low-temperature plasma synergistic catalysts. Low-temperature plasma stimulates high-energy electrons to dissociate reactants, thereby generating a large number of active free radicals. It can also increase the aromatization reactions and the activity of catalytic sites on the catalyst surface, which enhances the formation of hydrocarbons. The etching effect mainly comes from the positive ions, active free radicals, and active atoms generated in the system. These atoms can react with the active functional groups on the surface of the material and generate small molecular substances that can be volatilized. Therefore, the removal of weak functional groups on the surface of the carbon material can optimize the roughness and surface structure of the carbon material (Qiu et al., 2023).

The greater number of bonds are broken to form free radicals. In addition, some other neutral atoms and free radicals generated during the reaction will form a deposition layer on the surface of the material. Furthermore, surface roughness can reduce the contact angle θ of the material, which is conducive to improving the dispersion of the material in water. Cross-linking occurs because high-energy particles such as electrons, photons, excited particles, and free radicals present in the inert gas plasma destroy chemical bonds on the material surface through bombardment or certain chemical reactions. In the absence of other reactive species, the free radicals recombine to form a network by cross-linking with each other on the surface of the material. In the process of cross-linking reaction, there will be double building on the material surface, which highly enhances the mechanical and surface properties of the material. If the discharge gas is reactive, complex chemical reactions will occur on the activated surface of the material. Specific functional groups such as hydroxyl-PH, amino-NH2, carboxyl-COOH, and phthalimido-CONH were introduced on the surface through low-temperature plasma treatment, and as a result, it increased the affinity for water molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biowaste management strategies derived from plasma mixing systems
Phase change materials coupled with plasma-based and plasma-assisted techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The discard or improper disposal of biowaste causes environmental pollution, which ultimately leads to the wastage of valuable and potential secondary raw materials. To alleviate the energy crisis and environmental degradation, biowaste management is an important issue (Bhatia et al., 2018a). Based on the concepts of value-added waste and circular economy, the use of appropriate technologies to convert these biowastes into valuable materials or chemicals is of great importance (Xu et al., 2019a). Plasma technology has great potential to convert biowaste into useful recyclable products. Plasma pyrolysis or gasification technology emerged as a new method and has attracted great attention in recent years.

Pyrolysis is one of the thermochemical processes typically resulting in the reduction in the volume of biowaste and converting it into a useful form of energy and other value-added chemical products. It aims to convert biowaste into liquid bio-oil, solid carbon, and non-condensing gas at high temperatures in the absence of oxygen (Kan et al., 2016). However, while traditional pyrolysis technologies often suffer from low gas yields, a wide variety of products, and difficulty in separating, plasma pyrolysis is an innovative approach with high environmental and economic advantages in biowaste treatment (Tang et al., 2013). In this process, the plasma provides all the energy required for the decomposition of biowaste, rather than the energy generated by combustion. Traditional pyrolysis technology operates at low temperatures and slow heating rates, while plasma technology combines high temperatures and high heating rates to achieve destruction of biowaste (Huang & Tang, 2007). In addition, high-energy particles such as ions, electrons, and free radicals in the plasma enhance the breakdown of tar, ultimately generating cleaner products.

During the plasma pyrolysis process, volatile substances in biowaste are gradually converted into gaseous products, ultimately leading to a decrease in hydrogen and oxygen content, while the carbon content in the remaining solids increases sharply. In this method, carbonaceous materials are rapidly devolatilized at high heating rates, which is beneficial to the formation of highly porous carbon materials. Depending on the characteristics and operating conditions of different biowastes, gases and solid residues of various properties can be produced. In general, the desired product can be obtained by adjusting the input power and pressure. Additionally, operating at high input power and pressure will reduce the amount of solid residue obtained and increase gas yield and overall conversion in the process. For example, by controlling the operating pressure and input power in capacitively coupled radio frequency plasma pyrolysis, Tang et al. (Tang & Huang, 2005) achieved an average gas production rate of 66 % from biomass feedstock (mainly H2 and CO), and the resulting solid carbon has a large specific surface area and high porosity, which makes it a potential activated carbon. Besides, the temperature also affects the conversion of biowaste.

Complementarily, the composition of the gas produced depends on the percentage of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen present in the biowaste and the equilibrium state, so that when a certain temperature is reached, the temperature does not have a significant effect on the composition of the gas produced. For example, Muvhiiwa et al. (Muvhiiwa et al., 2018) pyrolyzed wood chips with electrical energy provided by a water-cooled nitrogen plasma torch. At temperatures of 400 ℃ and 600 ℃, the products contained bio-oil, biochar, and syngas. While at 800 ℃ and 1000 ℃, the tar was decomposed and the products consisted mainly of syngas and biochar. This demonstrates the effect of temperature on the conversion of biomass as well as on the yield of syngas. Recently, Stein et al. (Stein et al., 2022) developed an interesting study using fungal mycelium as a raw material to produce a “mycodiamond” matrix which basically contains ultra-crystalline diamond by using microwave plasma pyrolysis technique. This experiment leads to the sustainable transformation of biomass to diamond-based nanomaterials which is due to the presence of high temperature, local pressure, and plasma chemical reaction induced in the process of plasma pyrolysis.

Gasification is an advanced heat treatment technology used in the conversion of biowaste. It can usually convert solid or liquid carbonaceous materials into gaseous products by reacting with oxygen-containing gases. Gasification can be regarded as an extension of the pyrolysis technique. During the traditional incineration process, biowaste often undergoes oxidation reactions, leading to the formation of harmful pollutants commonly seen in this technology, such as nitrous oxide and dioxins, causing environmental pollution (Tendler et al., 2005). Compared to incineration, gasification is a low-oxygen process that produces cleaner gas, thereby converting low-value raw materials into marketable products and fuels (Tatarova et al., 2014). Since plasma can effectively break any type of chemical bond and quickly initiate various chemical reactions, plasma sources have been widely used in the gasification of biowaste (Tamošiūnas et al., 2017; Vecten et al., 2021).

Typically, conventional biowaste gasification is carried out at 500–1400 ℃ (Ruiz et al., 2013). The most used plasma gasification is based on thermal plasma technology, which usually works at atmospheric pressure and high temperatures like more than 5000 ℃ and finally has a high reaction rate. The technology provides conditions unattainable by traditional heat treatments and can destroy virtually any material of varying particle size, moisture content, and elemental composition. Molecular dissociation occurs within the temperature range of the plasma, pollutants and toxic substances are broken down, thus controlling the composition of the resulting gas and minimizing the formation of unwanted contaminants such as tar. (Arena, 2012). Theoretically, the simple gas composition of plasma pyrolysis is due to the short residence time and quenching time in the plasma, which limits the slow multistep reactions (Tendler et al., 2005).

Remarkably, if biowaste is heated to a suitable high temperature, all the carbon and hydrogen present in the organic matter can be used to produce fuel gas. When all carbon is converted to CO, the maximum conversion efficiency can be obtained. The addition of oxidants (i.e., air, oxygen, or steam) can avoid the generation of solid carbon and ensure the complete gasification of the biowaste. The production of syngas from biowaste by thermal plasma-assisted technology depends on many parameters, such as the nature of biowaste, residence time, reaction temperature, plasma power, and plasma gas. To explore the performance of plasma gasification of biowaste, Hlina et al. (Hlina et al., 2014) conducted plasma gasification experiments on sawdust (spruce) and waste plastics, which generates high-quality syngas (51 vol% CO and 42 vol% H2) with negligible amount of tar. Kuo et al. (Kuo et al., 2021b) used microalgae as raw materials for steam plasma gasification for the production of renewable hydrogen. Previous studies conclude that thermal plasma is a promising technology to convert biowaste into high-quality syngas.

However, some biowastes have inherent unfavorable characteristics such as high moisture content, large volume, and low calorific value. To overcome these problems, roasting pretreatment is introduced to change the chemical and physical properties of biowaste, thereby enabling better results from plasma gasification technology (Guo et al., 2017; Pinto et al., 2017). For example, Kuo et al. (Kuo et al., 2020) analyzed the plasma gasification characteristics of woody, non-woody, algal biomass, and torrefied biomass under three gasification agents including air, steam, and CO2. The results revealed that the highest values of plasma gasification efficiency were obtained when CO2 was used as the gasification agent. The torrefied biomass showed an increase in the production of syngas as well as the conversion efficiency via plasma gasification. In addition, since the high energy density of coal can improve the conversion rate of biowaste and the ability of alkali metals present in the biowaste to catalyze the coal gasification. It is expected that the co-gasification of biowaste and coal can enhance the efficiency of the utilization of raw materials (Song et al., 2019b). In the co-gasification process, the volatile material is easy to decompose to form free radicals and react with the organic matter present in the coal, which increases the conversion of raw material and reduces the emissions of CO2, SO2, and NOx. The content and yield of gaseous products can be optimized by changing the quantity, nature, and temperature of the feed mixture (Emami-Taba et al., 2013).

However, thermal plasma gasification technologies tend to require higher energy and face operational challenges related to extreme temperatures and the need for expensive, high-temperature-resistant materials. Non-thermal plasma technologies such as corona discharge, sliding arc discharge, dielectric barrier discharge, and microwave discharge can also provide promising solutions for the effective conversion of biowaste and low-temperature detarring (Mei et al., 2019). Indrawan et al. (Indrawan et al., 2019) developed a model of a low-temperature municipal solid waste plasma gasification system that reduces the energy consumption from 3816 kW at 4000 ℃ to 2358 kW at 1500 ℃. The system produced 46 vol% syngas consisting of H2 and CO, which can be used for power generation.

In addition, Tang et al. (Tang et al., 2010)showed that the installation of a “radio-frequency capacitance-coupled plasma thermal decomposition reactor” could successfully treat biological waste using plasma technology with an average gas production of 66 % and an average syngas concentration of 76 %. The typical capacity is 1800 t/year, the utilization rate is 0.68 (6000 h/year), the total electric power of the plasma thermal decomposition device is 240 kW, and the biomass raw material can be processed at a speed of 300 kg/h, and the annual consumption of syngas is 1200 t. Biomass costs $28/t and $0.05/kWh. Syngas costs $354/t for 1,500 t/year. The reactor costs $1.5 million to install and can be repaid for $300,000 over 15 years. This plasma combines the high reactivity and thermal efficiency of the plasma with the moderate temperature, which is conducive to the cracking of hydrocarbons, thus increasing the production of syngas. This result has practical significance for the production of gaseous fuel and charcoal from biomass materials.

In addition to plasma pyrolysis and gasification, other plasma technologies have been used to convert biowaste into carbon-based materials or gases, as shown in Table 5 . As a low-temperature plasma technology, microplasma has the characteristics of micro-scale geometric structure, high reaction rate, self-organization phenomenon, and non-equilibrium reaction environment. It is an effective method to obtain advanced materials in the gas phase and liquid phase (Chiang et al., 2020). The high-energy electron energy in microplasma can decompose raw materials without the support of vacuum equipment, producing high concentrations of active free radical species. These highly reactive free radicals collide with each other to form small clusters, which then undergo a series of processes to form nanoscale materials (Lin & Wang, 2015). The formation of these nanomaterials is closely related to parameters such as temperature, gas composition, input power, catalyst, and gas flow rate.

Recently, plasma liquefaction of biowaste in water or organic solvents at mild temperatures has been proposed as a method to convert biowaste into value-added fuels and chemicals through plasma-induced highly reactive chemical reactions. It turns out that non-thermal plasmas can be effectively applied to chemical transformations in relatively short periods of time and with low energy consumption (Mei et al., 2020). Complementarily, plasma electrolytic oxidation is the combination of plasma chemistry with electrochemistry which has shown high potential in the rapid catalytic conversion of biowaste (Wang et al., 2020b; Xi et al., 2022). Moreover, plasma technology helps in the acceleration of chemical reactions and can also be used to assist or enhance some reaction processes.

The syngas produced from biowaste due to plasma pyrolysis or gasification technology have fewer impurities, and the syngas formed during the process can be used to power fuel cells, gas turbines, or gas engines, and generate electricity for power plants. In order to improve energy utilization, directly connecting the plasma gasification system to multiple power plants is a potential and promising option to improve the overall performance of the biowaste power generation process and develop a circular economy with low investment.

Coupling plasma gasification with existing power systems can improve the overall performance of integrated gasification power systems and has great potential to promote environmentally sustainable development. Coal-fired power plants have the advantages of being reliable, efficient, affordable, and safe. Multiple sources of energy and plenty of energy conversion processes are involved in the power generation process. The combination of coal-fired power plants with other energy sources or thermal processes will improve overall energy efficiency. Pan et al. (Pan et al., 2022) investigated a hybrid system which integrates the gasification of biowaste and coal-fired power generation. This system delivers the syngas generated by thermal plasma gasification of municipal solid waste directly into the coal-fired boiler, so that the energy of the syngas can be used to generate electricity through the steam cycle of the coal-fired power plant. The net power generation efficiency of this system was 35.16 %, which could generate 16.12 MW net power. Through economic analysis, it only took 4.58 years to pay back the initial investment and was expected to achieve a net present value of 40,341.00 k$ in the 25-year of lifespan (Pan et al., 2022).

However, the existing fiscal incentives and financing mechanisms of the government in the energy and environment should be considered for the economic analysis. In Colombia, 30 % of landfills have a short life span. To avoid sanitary emergencies, Montiel-Bohórquez et al. (Montiel-Bohórquez et al., 2022) evaluated the techno-economic performance of an integrated plasma gasification combined circulation system (IPGCC) fueled by municipal solid waste in Colombia and conducted an in-depth study of the effects of plasma temperature and reactor temperature on this system. The results revealed that the IPGCC plant, which processes 900 tons of municipal solid waste per day, can generate approximately 56 MW of net electricity under optimal temperature conditions. The levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) is higher than the 2020 average electricity price in Colombia when charging a reliable cost of 1.70 ¢US$/kWh. However, if the investment tax credit is fully utilized during the first five years of the project, the LCOE is lower than the 2020 average electricity price and the project is feasible. Nonetheless, other strategies and policies need to be sought to improve the viability of the project in the country.

Among the present technologies for electricity production, solid oxide fuel cells (SOFC) are an efficient technology for converting chemical energy from fuel to electricity. Since the operating temperature of SOFC is close to the gasification temperature, plasma gasification technology can be integrated with SOFC, providing a promising way to develop an efficient biowaste power generation process. To maximize the performance of the SOFC system, Recalde et al. (Recalde et al., 2018) integrated SOFC with plasma gasification and developed a gasified SOFC power plant to efficiently recover the energy from fecal sludge. The simulation results show that the net electrical efficiency and energy efficiency of the system will reach 65.3 % and 57.8 %, respectively, proving that this hybrid system can not only generate clean electricity in a stable and efficient manner, but also has great potential for the long-term operation (Recalde et al., 2018). Perna et al. (Perna et al., 2018) designed an integrated air plasma gasification fuel cell (IAPGFC) for the conversion of municipal solid waste into energy and renewable electricity through three parts: solid oxide electrolyzer (SOEC), plasma technology, and SOFC. The results show that IAPGFC performs well in terms of energy storage efficiency (87.7–92.1 %), and the electrical efficiency of the IAPGFC system increases from 34.6 % to 40.1 %, both of which are higher than the 33 % of the traditional integrated waste treatment system (Perna et al., 2018).

Chemical cycle combustion (CLC) is an effective CO2 emission reduction strategy that has attracted worldwide attention in recent years and has been applied in power plants through different fuels. Jiang et al. (Jiang et al., 2021) proposed a two-stage fluidized bed plasma gasification power generation system combining SOFC and CLC. The simulation results exhibited that the CO2 capture efficiency, net electric efficiency, and energy efficiency of the hybrid system were 99 %, 41 %, and 36 %, respectively. Additionally, with other municipal solid waste by using incineration or gasification power generation processes, the net power efficiency of the process increased by at least 14 %. Apart from SOFC, plasma gasification can also couple with other fuel cells, such as molten carbonate fuel cells (MCFCs), with electrical efficiencies higher than those achieved with conventional refuse-derived fuel (RDF) incineration techniques (Falcucci et al., 2012).Plasma gasification has higher environmental benefits than other thermochemical technologies, especially in terms of emission reduction. Environmental impact assessments have identified the global warming, acidification, and dioxin emission potential of these technologies. Cudjoe and Wang (Cudjoe & Wang, 2022) calculated the environmental impact results, indicating that during the project, the global warming potential generated by plasma gasification is 62,879.8 kt·CO2eq, and the CO2eq generated by incineration is 62,900.5 kt·CO2eq. The total acidification potential over the life of the project is 32.1 million kg SO2eq for plasma gasification and 125.8 million kg·SO2eq for incineration.

Model building based on artificial intelligence (AI) can help us visualize the system according to style requirements and actual conditions, and reduce cost losses. Every system can be described by different models from various aspects. In plasma systems, it is crucial to optimize the experimental process to obtain a promising product with certain input parameters. However, system tuning and design based on laboratory experimental results is expensive and time-consuming. Model building predicts and adjusts experimental variables to address system performance, which is an optimization method that saves energy, resources, and time in the design and operation of plasma systems (Ibrahimoglu et al., 2017). For example, Ismail et al. (Ismail et al., 2019) proposed a numerical model that can simulate plasma gasification of forest residues with low computational power. This Euler model allows modifications depending on the type of reactor used. They demonstrated the robustness of the model based on collected literature data and concluded through parameter evaluation that lower equivalence ratios and higher bed temperatures are beneficial to the production of H2 and CO (Ismail et al., 2019).

Most of the studies mainly considered the specific type of plasma, feedstock, and operating conditions. Therefore, Chu et al. (Chu et al., 2022) used stepwise linear regression (SLR) and artificial neural network (ANN) methods for the development of the predictive models. These models were used to simulate the relationship between some input parameters and syngas characteristics in the plasma gasification of municipal solid waste, providing general guidance for the design of the plasma gasification. These models confirm that requirements in the specific energy and input power are the key parameters affecting the low heating value and volume fraction of H2, CO, and CH4. Among them, the relative importance of the input power on the H2 and CO were 100 vol% and 82 vol%, respectively, which indicates that adjusting the input power can provide an effective strategy for increasing the H2 ratio in the syngas. In addition to the optimization of plasma system parameters, Mountouris et al. (Mountouris et al., 2006) proposed an equilibrium gasification model, called GasifEq, which focuses on thermodynamic data and energy and flame usage in recent plasma gasification technology to predict the performance of the plasma gasification process.

Besides, the model construction can also be applied to the mechanistic investigation to give a better understanding of the plasma technology process. For instance, Huang et al. (Huang et al., 2016) clarified the reaction mechanism for the pyrolysis of hemicellulose in the hydrogen plasma through density functional theory (DFT) and has proposed the most favorable reaction path, and also discussed the influence of biomass composition on the formation of product and distribution of the yield. Interestingly, the construction of the model is not only helpful for the construction of the overall system, but can also focus on the specific structural design of the system. Thevenin and coworkers (Thevenin et al., 2014) have combined the modeling with experiments to simulate the chemical environment and heat distribution in a biomass gasification reactor through kinetics and thermochemistry, thereby selecting the refractory material suitable for the refractory lining of the reactor to resist the chemical and thermal corrosion. The study demonstrated that the best results were obtained with alumina and chromium oxide for the upper section of the reactor and high alumina for the reaction chamber and ashtray (Thevenin et al., 2014).

Model building has great advantages in the development of complex energy coupling systems because it can not only design and optimize the system to ensure high performance, but also accurately analyze the rationality and feasibility of the system to avoid unnecessary investment and energy waste. Kuo et al. (Kuo et al., 2021a) established a new high-efficiency waste-to-energy power plant model system (T-PG-SOFC-CHP) using refuse-derived fuel as feedstock, including plasma gasifier, torrefaction, SOFC, and combined heat and power. Through thermodynamic evaluation, it is concluded that mild baking temperatures of 200 °C and 250 °C are more beneficial to the electrical efficiency and cogeneration efficiency of the system, but the input energy results in a lower return on energy investment.

In addition, the system introduces oxy-fuel combustion technology, which will result in an additional energy loss of 5.40–6.77 %, but can achieve no direct CO2 emissions (Kuo et al., 2021a). This model still needs to be further refined by considering geometric and dynamic parameters. Peng et al. (Peng et al., 2021) developed a conceptual hybrid waste-to-energy system combining plasma gasification, gas turbine, SOFC, supercritical CO2 cycle, and waste heat recovery. Through simulation, it is concluded that the net waste in the efficiency of power generation in the hybrid system is 59.30 %. The cogeneration efficiency reaches 87.81 % while the total energy efficiency achieves 57.56 %. Additionally, the economic analysis demonstrates that the system has a low investment of 29322.49 k$, a 20-year lifetime NPV of 109815.39 k$, and a dynamic payback period of only 3.77 years. Therefore, this new system is highly feasible and effective from a thermodynamic and economic point of view, which provides a reference for waste disposal and CHP energy recovery technologies (Peng et al., 2021).

Compared with other traditional carbon materials, BCMs are economical and feasible for energy and environmental applications. Therefore, the applications of BCMs have been extensively studied, and the methods of converting BCMs for energy conversion, energy storage, biomedical and biotechnological applications have been reviewed and summarized.

With the continuous progress of the scientific field and the development of society, there is an urgent need for new energy and environmental strategies to control pollution, which often requires high-efficiency chemical reactions (Chen et al., 2021a). According to the statistics, at least 80 % of the reaction efficiency in the chemical process depends on the catalysts (Cui et al., 2018). In recent years, carbon-based materials prepared from biowaste have been widely studied in the field of energy and environmental catalysis due to their low cost, easy availability, environmental friendliness and abundant active sites in their structures.

Heavy reliance on the use of fossil fuels not only aggravates environmental pollution but also inevitably leads to an energy crisis. Therefore, developing clean, cheap, and sustainable energy as an alternative to non-renewable fossil fuels has attracted widespread attention. Hydrogen is widely regarded as an ideal green renewable energy source with the characteristics of high combustion value, high energy density, light weight, and no pollution to the environment (Oluigbo et al., 2020). Water splitting is an important way to achieve simple, green, cheap, and large-scale industrial production of hydrogen (Yao et al., 2020). The half-cell reaction on the cathode, called the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), requires two electron transport reactions (Kulkarni et al., 2023). The HER mechanism occurs in two steps, pH-dependent on surface phenomena. Additionally, the HER mechanism mainly involves the reduction of a universal solvent (∼pH-14) in an alkaline medium or a proton reduction (∼pH-1) in an acidic medium (depending on the electrolyte) throughout the catalytic process. The HER reaction mechanism occurs in two ways, the Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer-Tafel process, usually depending on the type of solvent used for the electrochemical reaction and the physicochemical properties of the electrocatalyst, as shown in Eq (1–3) below:

To significantly improve water-splitting efficiency, Pt-based catalysts are currently the most effective HER catalysts. The usage of Pt-based catalyst makes it difficult to achieve the large-scale production of hydrogen and its usage in different applications because of the high price and scarcity of the Pt element (Feng et al., 2021). Therefore, it is important to develop a new HER catalyst with high activity and economic feasibility.

With the continuous in-depth research of BCMs in the field of HER, heteroatom doping is an effective method to improve the catalytic HER performance with the help of BCMs. In addition to exogenous atoms that dope chemical reagents, the abundant heteroatoms present in biowaste can form self-doping atoms, thereby overall improving catalytic performance while reducing the use of chemical reagents (Wang et al., 2021c). Prabu et al. (Prabu et al., 2019) prepared graphene-like hierarchical porous nanosheets (HPNS) by carbonization and KOH activation using the biowaste of palm plants as a precursor. This material has a high nitrogen content (10.5 %) from the original biomass and a high specific surface area of 1297 m2/g. HPNS exhibited wonderful HER activity with a lower overpotential (η10) of −0.33 V, a smaller Tafel slope value of 63 mV•dec-1, and long-term durability in the acidic media.

Compared with single-element doped graphene, double-doped graphene exhibits more incredible properties in the application of HER. Ma et al. (Ma et al., 2021) constructed N and P double-doped biochar by hydrothermal carbonization and pyrolysis using biowaste as a precursor. The results showed that N and P doping can form favorable electronic structures and induce a variety of defect sites in the biochar structure. In addition to N and P co-doped carbon materials, the doping of other non-metallic elements such as S and B can also be beneficial for the electrocatalytic activity of HER via building more defects and exposing more active sites (Mulyadi et al., 2017). In addition, combining non-noble metals (e.g., Co, Cu, Fe, Mo, and Ni) and their oxides (e.g., carbides, sulfides, and phosphides) can independently catalyze HER, which can enhance the conductivity and plasticity of the BCM. Besides, it can provide BCM with higher hydrogen adsorption energy and better electronic structure, which can be used as an economical new electrocatalytic material (Min et al., 2020).

Among the non-noble metal compounds, molybdenum carbide (Mo2C) is one of the most promising HER electrocatalysts due to its similar electronic properties with noble metal catalysts (e.g. Pt, Pd) (Ma et al., 2020). Fakayode et al. (Fakayode et al., 2021) fabricated the electrocatalyst Mo2C/C with watermelon rind, fumaric acid, and molybdenum metal. The synthesized electrocatalyst exhibited excellent performance under the current density of 10 mA•cm−2 in 1.0 m KOH with the overpotential of 133 mV for 300 h. Besides, it has been proved that the synergistic effect of the doped heteroatoms and non-noble metals effectively improves the catalytic activity of BCMs (Hoang et al., 2020; Upadhyay & Pandey, 2020).

Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is a type of reaction in which oxygen has been used as a reactant to generate either water (H2O) or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) via either a four-electron pathway or a two-electron pathway. For typical ORR reactions, there are usually two kinds of two-electron ORR process and four-electron ORR process (Ma et al., 2019). The two-electron ORR uses O2 as a reactant to generate H2O2 for use in wastewater treatment processes or the addition of chemicals, while the four-electron ORR process can fully reduce O2 to H2O for greater energy efficiency. The four-electron ORR process is divided into dissociative pathways and non-dissociative pathways. It occurs when the surface oxygen coverage degree is relatively low or the adsorption of O2 is strong, and when the surface oxygen coverage degree is relatively high or the adsorption of O2 is weak. Taking the non-dissociative pathway as an example, the ORR reaction will go through the following processes:
