It is extensively used in energy conversion, energy storage, and chemical technology (Yang et al., 2021). The ORR process for the H2O generation has been incorporated in many bio-electrochemical devices, especially fuel cells and metal-air cells (Zhao et al., 2022). The ORR process involved in this kind of process tends to have slow reaction kinetics, which is not favorable in practical applications (Yan et al., 2021). Currently, the noble metal platinum (Pt) has the highest intrinsic activity among numerous catalysts available for the ORR process. Besides, Pt-based catalyst has achieved excellent results in energy conversion (Wang et al., 2020c). However, the inherent scarcity and high price of Pt-based catalysts make the overall cost of manufacturing very high for large-scale applications. BCM is considered a promising alternative to noble metal catalysts in ORR applications. In addition to low cost and large-scale availability, BCM is electrically conductive and corrosion-resistant.

Activation of chemical reagents such as KOH, H3PO4, or ZnCl2 is often used in the preparation of BCM, mainly because it directly affects the pore structure of BCM and changes the structural properties of BCM. The electrocatalytic performance of BCM mainly depends on its surface active sites, conductivity, the presence of atomic dopants and hydrophilic functional groups, which facilitate the entry of electrolyte into the pores and facilitate more contact between oxygen and catalyst (Kaur et al., 2019). Among the various doped nonmetallic atoms, Si is a relatively rare atom. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2022) developed an ORR electrocatalyst for zinc-air batteries using rice husk as a precursor and ZnCl2 as an activating reagent. The potential observed with this catalyst was 0.89 V and the onset potential was 0.96 V, which is better than the benchmark Pt/C catalyst. It was found that the self-doped Si present in the rice husk not only promoted the formation of functional groups like pyridine N, graphite N, and Fe-Nx, but also improved the stability of the catalyst (Wang et al., 2022).

Due to the unique electronic properties and electrical conductivity of Co and N co-doped carbon materials, Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2022) studied a novel composite Co/N-C from peanut shells and used the catalyst in electrochemistry. The Co-N heteroatom present in this catalyst triggered the abundant Co-N active sites with a half-wave potential of 0.83 V and an ultimate current density of 5.45 mA/cm2, with enhanced ORR catalytic ability and stability in 0.1 M KOH. Notably, ORR kinetics is strongly dependent on the pH and is inhibited in near-neutral media (6 < pH<9) (Santoro et al.). To date, there are relatively few studies on synthetic ORR catalytic materials in acidic and neutral media. And the catalytic efficiency is lower than that in the alkaline media (Chai et al., 2017).

With the development of industry and the expansion of human activities, carbon dioxide emissions are increasing day by day, affecting global climate conditions and posing a great threat to human living conditions. The conversion of CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels such as CO, methanol, ethanol, and formic acid by an electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) is one of the most attractive ways to use CO2 in a sustainable way (Wang et al., 2021b). CO2RR is often challenging due to the stable chemical nature of CO2. BCMs have made an impressive contribution in the study for efficient and selective CO2RR electrocatalysts. The large surface area of BCMs facilitates the exposure of more active sites. Besides, the structures of mesopores and macropores present on the surface of BCMs facilitate the transport of protons and electrons. And the abundant micropores present on its surface allow better CO2 capture (Li et al., 2018).

Among the various products of CO2RR, CO is one of the most readily produced by electrocatalytic CO2 reduction in an aqueous electrolyte at atmospheric pressure and can be used as a feedstock for syngas. Yao et al. (Yao et al., 2019) prepared N-doped nanoporous carbon sheets with abundant pores and high surface area from cattail used as the raw material. The high pyridine N structures present in the carbon catalyst reduced the free energy barrier of COOH· and achieved higher CO selectivity (90 %) at an overpotential of 0.31 V (Yao et al., 2019). Gong and coworkers (Gong et al., 2021) have prepared the carbon aerogels using silkworm cocoons as starting material combined with the trace of copper nanoparticles using zinc nitrate as a pore-forming agent. The results show that this process not only enriched the active sites, but also accelerated the rate of electron transfer and desorption of •CO intermediates, thereby promoting the CO2 transform. The catalyst achieved an excellent current density of 29.4 mA•cm−2 with a Faradaic efficiency of 83 % for CO (Gong et al., 2021).

BCMs can also be used as an effective catalyst for photocatalytic CO2RR. For example, Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2021a) prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using waste orange peel by hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the CQDs were modified with the TiO2 nanoparticles and used in the photocatalytic activity. This material combines the unique up-conversion effect and electron library properties of CQDs with the excellent photocatalytic properties of TiO2 nanotubes such as the narrow band gap and strong photocurrent. The results show that the highest yields of CO and CH4 were 13.55 and 3.54 μmol•g−1, respectively, with the combination of CQDs and TiO2 as catalysts (Zhang et al., 2021a). In addition to CO, oxalic acid can also be formed as one of the products in the CO2RR process. And it is a versatile acid with great commercial and industrial potential. Costa et al. (Costa et al., 2020) used coconut mesocarp as raw material, hydrothermal carbonization in the presence of silver nitrate to synthesize a carbon/silver catalyst, and finally obtained oxalic acid with a Faradaic efficiency of 28.95 % through high conversion of CO2 (Costa et al., 2020). Nonetheless, there are still few studies on the catalytic reduction of CO2 to other valuable chemicals by BCM. And the possibility of BCM in CO2RR still needs to be investigated.

The development of energy conversion and storage devices with high power and high energy density is crucial for reducing dependence on fossil fuels and for the sustainable development of society. The use of high-performance materials in devices is key to progress in this field. As a new, low-cost, environmentally friendly material, BCM is being incorporated into many energy devices and has broad application prospects in energy fields such as supercapacitors, batteries, and photovoltaics.

Supercapacitors, as a promising energy storage device, have received considerable attention over time due to their fast charge–discharge rate, long lifetime, and excellent energy density. According to the energy storage mechanism, supercapacitors are generally classified into electric double-layer capacitors (EDLC) and pseudocapacitors (Wang et al., 2020b). The energy storage mechanisms of both type of supercapacitors depend on the properties of the electrodes. Electrode materials are an integral part for the development of high-efficiency supercapacitors. Among the various electrode materials, BCMs have gained great popularity owing to their low cost, tunable porosity, high electrical conductivity, and easy processing properties.

Different biowastes have been extensively developed for supercapacitor applications, as shown in Table 6 . Since the alkaline or alkaline earth metals present in the lotus calyx contribute to the formation of pores in the carbon material. Dhakal and co-workers (Dhakal et al., 2022) have reported the three-dimensional (3D) porous carbon prepared by simultaneous activation and carbonization of the lotus calyx biowaste. The designed electrodes have a specific capacitance of 223F•g−1 at 1 A•g−1 and a large specific surface area of 798 m2•g−1, maintaining 97 % of the initial capacitance even after 50,000 cycles in the KOH electrolyte (Dhakal et al., 2022). The characteristics of BCMs exhibited in the supercapacitors are not only related to the nature of the biowaste itself, but also on its synthesis route. Song et al. (Song et al., 2019a) used mung bean husks as precursors for the generation of porous carbon materials via the process of hydrothermal or pyrolytic synthesis. The results illustrated that the carbon materials prepared with the hydrothermal method were 3D honeycomb structures with a high specific surface area of 1214 m2•g−1 and high amount of surface functionality with a specific capacitance of 353F•g−1 at 1 A•g−1.

It has been reported that oxygen-containing functional groups can gradually enhance the capacitance with an increase in the electrochemical activity (Conway et al., 1997). In addition, activation is a common method to increase the porosity as well as to improve the specific surface area in the carbon materials, which is usually divided into physical, chemical, and biological activation. Adan-Mas et al. (Adan-Mas et al., 2021) used coffee waste to prepare the activated carbon by hydrothermal carbonization. The results showed that the physically activated carbon exhibited a more graphitized cracked micromorphology, while the chemically activated carbon showed a highly porous microstructure with a high specific surface area of 2330 m2•g−1. The chemically activated carbon performs better, with a capacitance value of 84F•g−1 at 1 A•g−1 in the voltage range of 0.8–1.1 V and a capacitance retention of 85 % after 5000 consecutive charges and discharges (Adan-Mas et al., 2021). Combining BCMs with other materials with high energy density and charge transport efficiency to form composites has also shown great potential in the field of high-performance supercapacitors (Ji et al., 2020).

With the current rapid development of wearable and bendable electronics, there is an increasing demand for lightweight and flexible supercapacitors as they can be well integrated with wearable systems. Haj et al. (Li et al., 2019) extracted cellulose nanocrystals from brewery residues for the electrolyte, while animal bone biowaste was used to synthesize the porous carbon electrodes. This carbon catalyst has been demonstrated for the first time in the preparation of flexible symmetrical supercapacitors from biowaste. The device has an energy density of 18.2 Wh•kg−1 at 1425 W•kg−1 and a power density of 20833 W•kg−1 at 7.1 Wh•kg−1 while the capacitance remains at 92 % after 6000 cycles at 5 A•g−1 (Al Haj et al., 2022). The excellent performance of BCMs also holds great promise for flexible solid-state supercapacitors (Li et al., 2019).

As a traditional energy storage device, a battery aims to store energy in the form of chemical energy and convert it into electrical energy for the power supply. According to the classification of electrode materials, batteries can be divided into lithium-ion batteries, zinc-manganese batteries, lead-acid batteries, nickel–cadmium batteries, etc. As a prominent example, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have revolutionized human activities in modern society (Manthiram, 2020). Xuan et al. (Kwon et al., 2020) developed a silicon-carbon (Si-C) hybrid composite anode for high-energy LIBs using corn starch biowaste and silicon nanoparticles by using a scalable microemulsion method. The electrode consists of silicon nanoparticles embedded in the carbon spheres and externally wrapped with a graphitic carbon coating. The catalyst shows a high capacity (1800 mAh•g−1), high capacity retention after 500 cycles (80 %), and fast charge/discharge capability (12 min) (Kwon et al., 2020).

However, as conventional LIBs have been extensively studied and developed, the energy density is approaching the theoretical limit. Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries, which use sulfur as the anode and lithium metal as the cathode, have received much attention in recent years because of their ultra-high energy density (2600 Wh･kg−1) (Fang et al., 2019). BCMs are promising candidates for sulfur storage hosts by virtue of their high surface area and excellent adsorption of polysulfides (Yuan et al., 2019b). Tesio et al. (Tesio et al., 2020) used biowaste from beer brewing for the production of carbon materials by pyrolysis method with a high sulfur loading of 70 % and further used this material as cathodes in the Li-S batteries. The capacity loss was as low as 0.01 % per cycle after 400 cycles at the high current (98 mAh•g−1 at 5 ℃). Atomic doping can enhance the transfer and utilization of electrons and ions from the active sulfur in the material. Ren et al. (Ren et al., 2019) synthesized an in-situ N, O, and P triple-doped 3D layered porous carbon by carbonization and activation with H3PO4 using goat hair as a precursor. With a high initial discharge capacity of 1185 mA h•g−1 at 0.05 ℃ and 489 mAh•g−1 of stable capacity after 300 cycles at 0.2 ℃, it exhibits excellent electrochemical performance in the positive electrode of Li-S batteries.

Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) and potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) are considered as viable alternatives to lithium-ion batteries as a result of low cost, non-toxicity, and more readily available sodium and potassium resources on the planet (Dhir et al., 2020; Vaalma et al., 2018). Rath et al. (Rath et al., 2019) extracted hard carbon from the abundant source of sugarcane bagasse for the NIBs anode, which had high first-cycle CE (70 %) and good cyclability (94 % retention after 300 cycles), which attributed to the less defects, low oxygen content, more sp2-hybridized carbon, and unique lamellar morphology (Rath et al., 2019). Verma et al. (Verma et al., 2020) fabricated a mesoporous carbon from the orange peel and used this carbon as an anode material for the PIBs. The material had an initial discharge/charge capacity of 408/224 mAh•g−1 at 30 mA•g−1 and was able to maintain a reversible capacity of 208.57 mAh•g−1 over 100 cycles. Furthermore, BCMs derived from the diverse biowaste have been incorporated into metal-air batteries as popular materials for the electrocatalyst carriers and gas diffusion layers due to their excellent catalytic activity, pore abundance, and large specific surface area (Senthil & Lee, 2021).

Photovoltaic (PV) is the most sustainable way to convert renewable solar energy into electric energy without producing any kind of pollution in the environment, for energy storage, conversion, and management. However, it is important to significantly reduce the overall cost of the solar conversion process to make PV dominant in the electricity market (Polman et al., 2016). The development of biowaste-based cavity transport materials has attracted considerable interest in the field of PV.

However, biochar (BC) derived from various biowaste sources often lacks excellent electrocatalytic activity. To overcome this obstacle, Sumathi et al. (Sumathi et al., 2022) improved the material stability and catalytic activity by combining aloe-derived BC with advanced nickel molybdenum sulfide (NiMoS3). The NiMoS3/BC composite has an energy band of 2.81 eV. In addition, the NiMoS3/BC-based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have a power conversion efficiency of 8.85 ± 0.003 % with low charge transfer resistance. Importantly, among the BCMs, carbon dots (CDs) materials are showing great properties in terms of photon absorption and electron excitation. And CDs are found in diverse PVs, such as DSSCs, organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells, and more recently in the calcium titanite solar cells (PSCs) (Paulo et al., 2016).

CDs are mainly classified into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbide polymer dots (CPDs) (Xia et al., 2019). Meng et al. (Meng et al., 2017) used soybean meal as raw material to controllably synthesize CQDs through a hydrothermal method, and combined them with long-afterglow phosphors with light storage/luminescence properties to construct a DSSC suitable for power generation in various weather conditions. The photoelectric conversion efficiency of up to 8 % can be also achieved under dark conditions (Meng et al., 2017). In a recent study, to address the instability of chalcogenide (PVK) materials on ZnO electron transfer layers (ETLs) during annealing, Ahmed and his co-workers (Ahmed et al., 2020) have prepared GQDs using corn flour as a precursor and then synthesized a hybrid ZnO/GQDs photovoltaic electrode as ETLs in PSCs. In particular, the GQDs can both passivate the oxygen atom on the surface of ZnO which leads to the reduction in the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus improving the overall stability. The results point out that the power conversion efficiency of the PSCs reaches 17 %, and the PSCs can still maintain a high initial efficiency when stored for 1500 h under the conditions of 60 % relative humidity and 80 °C, which is environmentally and economically feasible (Ahmed et al., 2020).

Among the BCMs, CDs are excellent fluorescent materials that stand out from lighting and display technologies owing to their good photoluminescence properties and easily controlled emission colors. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2021b) successfully prepared red, blue and green carbon dots (CDs) for multicolor Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) through a solvothermal pathway with gallic acid, which is commonly found in most of the fruits as well as in the plants. These three color-emitting CDs were coated with epoxy resin composites on LED chips, which were able to emitt light sources in the three colors. Also, LEDs which emits the white light can be prepared by mixing (CIE=0.32, 0.34) (Chen et al., 2021b). CPDs are a class of CDs with polymeric characteristics that have higher biocompatibility and lower cytotoxicity than conventional CDs.

Meng et al. (Meng et al., 2021) prepared two different CPDs that exhibit bright blue and blue-green fluorescence by hydrothermal reaction using avocado peel and sarcocarp as carbon sources. The two CPDs mixed with epoxy resin can construct blue and blue-green LEDs and also have the potential to fabricate white LEDs with high CRI (90.47 and 84.54, respectively), as shown in Fig. 4 (Meng et al., 2021). GQD is composed of one or more layers of graphene, combining the properties of quantum dots and graphene, which can also be used in LEDs (Yan et al., 2019). Roy et al. (Roy et al., 2014) developed the GQDs with high photostability and pH stability derived from the plant leaves using a hydrothermal method. White light conversion caps fabricated by hybrid technology using the GQDs as light converters can be used to generate white light from near-ultraviolet (UV) LEDs. Interestingly, Singh et al. (Singh et al., 2020) synthesized a self-assembled 2D array of CDs using human hair as a precursor and demonstrated a flexible organic LED device with a maximum emission of 350 cd•m−2 at 8 V for the first time.

With the development of biotechnology and the improvement of the medical level, biowaste-derived carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide, carbon dots, and other BCMs are being used for their inherent high biocompatibility, low biotoxicity, and fluorescence properties.

Drug delivery provides a possibility to develop new drugs, which can be delivered to the target sites through carriers (May 2022; Vargason et al., 2021). Among these carriers, BCMs are the most promising candidates owing to biocompatibility, abundant pore structure, and large surface area. These characteristics make them the best-fitted drug carriers and controlled-release materials on the target sites. Biowaste-based CDs have been extensively studied in this field.

CDs have the characteristics of high fluorescence intensity, good biocompatibility, small size, good stability, and easy surface functionalization, so these carbon materials have great potential in drug loading and drug release (Meng et al., 2019). Shao et al. (Shao et al., 2020) synthesized fluorescent CDs using mulberry leaf residues and loaded the anticancer drug Lycroine onto the CDs via van der Waals interactions or weak chemical bonds. The vitro experiments confirmed that the CDs had low biological toxicity to the human normal cells, and the Lycroine-CDs showed favorable anti-cancer activity. Generally, CDs with high nitrogen content have a positive surface charge and can enter cancer cells rapidly through electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged cell membranes (Feng et al., 2016). Besides, as a result of outstanding fluorescence properties, GQDs can be used as in situ labeling probes to track and detect the changes at the cellular level, thus integrating both fluorescence imaging diagnostic and cancer treatment strategies with a single drug type.

Thakur et al. (Thakur et al., 2017) prepared withered leaves-derived GQDs, which showed positive therapeutic effects for the breast cancer cells. In biocompatibility tests, cell viability exceeded 80 % even at a quantity of 250 μg•mL−1 of GQDs. The GQDs can be used for photothermal and photodynamic therapy as well as can be helpful in the tracking of cell death (Thakur et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2020c). Furthermore, pH-responsive drug release is necessary since the pH of the tumor mass environment (5.6–7.0) is different from that of normal tissues and blood (about 7.4) (Qiao et al., 2019). GQDs can also serve as suitable pH-responsive drug carriers for tumor therapy. In one of the studies, the anticancer drug was continuously released in a pH-responsive manner with a drug loading efficiency of 88 % due to bond cleavage between GQDs and the anticancer drug berberine hydrochloride complex at the acidic pH (Fig. 5 ) (Thakur et al., 2016). The materials that can be used in BCMs for drug delivery and cancer therapy are by no means limited to CDs. For example, Ma et al. (Ma et al., 2017) used waste camellia oleifera shells for the design of porous carbon microsphere with the characteristic of strong biological affinity by KOH activation and hydrothermal method, which has been used for the sustained release of the 5-fluorouracil which is an anticancer drug. In addition to the drug delivery of cancer therapeutics, biowaste-based CDs are extremely promising as the drug carriers for other diseases, such as antibacterial, antiviral and non-communicable diseases (Nair et al., 2020).

The urgent need for early detection and diagnosis of diseases has driven the prosperity of bioimaging techniques (Han et al., 2019). Bioimaging has become an invaluable tool to obtain various information about organisms in a rapid and detailed manner. CDs, carbon CNTs, and graphene oxide prepared from the biowaste as a starting material are promising candidates as a new generation of bioimaging probes due to their excellent optical properties.

CDs exhibit various characteristics like low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and fluorescence tunability in biological systems, usually with a size of less than 10 nm (Ali et al., 2020). Based on the tunable excitation-emission property, CDs with different excitation wavelengths can be used for multicolor cell imaging. Blue, green, and red fluorescence were obtained by excitation of the CDs at the wavelengths of 405, 488, and 543 nm, respectively. The survival rate of A375 cells was still more than 89 % even after increasing the concentration of CDs, which clearly proves that imaging of the polychromatic cells can be performed successfully (Sahoo et al., 2020).

Most studies have been devoted to obtaining high-quality red/near-infrared emitting CDs. It has been observed that the wavelength is related to the tissue penetration, and the emission of the shorter wavelength always overlaps with the fluorescent background of the organism which actually interferes with the imaging (Dordevic et al., 2022). Based on this, most of the current studies are devoted to obtaining high-quality red/near-infrared emitting CDs. Reagen et al. (Reagen et al., 2021) employed a bottom-up approach to preparing strongly fluorescent GQDs using biowaste derived from pesticide residue. A near-infrared region at about 860 nm was found in the spectrum. The vitro imaging showed that the GQDs were able to enter the mouse macrophage cell line of MCF-7 and mouse macrophage line RAW-246.7 through endocytosis, and were non-toxic to the cells with the concentrations of 200 μg•mL−1 of GQDs.

The addition of reagents has been used in the functionalization of CDs which improves the fluorescence imaging. Among them, nitrogen-doped CDs exhibit promising fluorescence properties with high quantum yields. For instance, Atchudan et al. (Atchudan et al., 2020) reported the hydrophilic nature of nitrogen-doped CDs by using the dwarf banana peel, which was prepared as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, fluorescent inks, and multicolor bioimaging (Fig. 6
a). Also, the toxicity of such CDs to Colon 9 hepatocytes cells is minimal, with the negligible changes in cell viability even at concentrations up to 200 μg•mL−1 (Atchudan et al., 2020). Furthermore, graphene and its oxides have been used for photoluminescence due to edge defects, isolated polyaromatic structures, and quantum confinement. Tewari et al. (Tewari et al., 2019) synthesized a bright blue fluorescent potassium-doped graphene oxide from agricultural waste. The viability of tumorigenic ovarian epithelial IOSE-364 cells was more than 90 % with a graphene oxide concentration of 30 μg•mL−1 (Tewari et al., 2019). Moreover, Singh and co-workers (Singh et al., 2021) have prepared highly fluorescent multi-walled CNTs using chickpea peel waste as a precursor, which exhibited favorable fluorescence properties in the cytoplasm of 22RV1 cells without any cytotoxicity (Singh et al., 2021). In vitro imaging, BCMs have also displayed significant potential for applications in living animals such as mice, as shown in Fig. 6 b (Zhang et al., 2018).

Sensors with the characteristics of low detection limits, high sensitivity, and selectivity are of great advantage in the detection of ions and molecules, as well as in the diagnosis of diseases. Like bioimaging, BCMs with excellent conductivity, water solubility, anti-bleaching, optical properties, and strong luminescence show tremendous potential for applications in sensing.

So far, BCMs have been used to fabricate diverse sensors to detect various ions, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ag+, I-, and NO2–. These sensors have been developed mainly by exploiting the affinity of certain functional groups of BCMs for the specific ions based on the fluorescence burst phenomenon. As a typical example, Fe3+ is the most frequently detected ion, which can combine with common functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, resulting in fluorescence quenching (Meng et al., 2019). BCMs prepared from biowaste such as lychee waste (Sahoo et al., 2020), empty fruit bunches (Saengsrichan et al., 2022), coffee grounds (Crista et al., 2020), and citrus limetta organic waste (Thakur et al., 2019) have been demonstrated for the Fe3+ detection. For example, Krishnaiah et al. (Krishnaiah et al., 2022) synthesized hydrophilic nature nitrogen-doped CDs with poa pratensis as a carbon source, which can be applied as a fluorescent probe for the sensing of Mn2+/ Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection of this material was 1.4 and 1.2 μM for Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, respectively, which is attributed to the fluorescence quenching caused by the generation of coordination bonds between the oxygen/nitrogen-containing moieties on the surface of nitrogen-doped CDs and Fe3+/Mn2+ ions (Krishnaiah et al., 2022).

BCMs also play an important role in the detection of biomarkers except for ions, which can be used for the risk detection of cancers and other diseases. Pandey et al. (Pandey et al., 2021) used corn husk-derived porous carbon to detect Fibronectin, which is a biomarker for ovarian cancer. This sensor has high selectivity and stability with a LOD of 129 fg mL−1. Sulfur doping provides an efficient pathway for the electron transfer process. Shahzad et al. (Kumawat et al., 2017; Shahzad et al., 2017) used mushrooms to prepare a sulfur-doped graphene oxide for the examination of 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine, an important biomarker in the cancer risk, exhibiting good selectivity, stability, and reproducibility (Kumawat et al., 2017). Interestingly, BCMs can also be used as temperature-sensing probes by exploiting the change in fluorescence intensity with a temperature change.

The complex structures present in nature have provided researchers with a broad range of ideas for designing artificial advanced materials with excellent properties (Vijayan & Puglia, 2019; Wegst et al., 2015). Therefore, bionic technology has always been the focus of research. To solve the challenges in the manufacturing faced by the bionic technology, the controllable synthesis and excellent performance of the BCMs provide a new strategy in the field of bionic technology.

Bionic design is mainly inspired by the analysis and imitation of the relationship between the function and structure of natural organisms. Natural structures are often arranged in microstructures or highly complex hierarchical structures that often integrate with multiple functions (Zhang et al., 2016). By mimicking the structural features of plant stems, Chen et al. (Cheng et al., 2020b) prepared a carbonaceous nanofibrous sponge with a lamellar structure from the wood-derived carbon nanofibers by a freeze-induced strategy. The layered structure and interconnected bridges of this biomimetic material provide extraordinary strength and elasticity, 1.5 to 5 times higher than the conventional materials, which offers an excellent choice for the development of lightweight, flexible wearable electronic devices and smart systems. Super hydrophobicity is one of the biological properties that widely exist in nature, such as in the lotus flowers.

Inspired by the eye of a mosquito due to its surface structure and superhydrophobic properties, Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2019c) developed a conductive biomimetic superhydrophobic material by controllably carbonizing wheat straw into a carbon-based material with a micro/nano-level structure. The favorable mechanical properties and water-repellent nature of the coatings prepared from this material have been demonstrated for the artificial rainfall tests. And the material was able to maintain structural stability even under the harsh environment. In another study, Sun et al. (Sun et al.) prepared a porous biomass carbon material with a pleated structure similar to the striated pleated structure of the canine maxillary turbinate by the direct carbonization of waste rose tea. This carbon-based material was applied as a bionic material for the high-sensitivity gas sensor. The results were observed with the ultra-high response of 60.16 k% to 500 ppm NH3 and the examination limit of 4.82 ppb. Moreover, bionic technology plays a vital role in the design of composite materials with characteristics such as self-healing, shape memory, self-growth, and self-healing.

3D printing, as additive manufacturing (AM), enables the layer-by-layer construction of digital models fabricated by computer-aided design (CAD) into 3D products (Li et al., 2020a). This advanced technology is driving significant innovation in numerous fields. In recent years, with the increasing demand for product complexity and functional diversity, many high-performance materials, including BCMs, have been used in 3D printing (Fig. 7 ).

The introduction of different fillers can improve the performance of printed products thus, facilitating the printing process. BCMs are an attractive candidate for fillers based on their unique characteristics such as excellent thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, and tensile properties. Alhelal et al. (Alhelal et al., 2021) used waste coffee grounds-derived biochar as a reinforcing additive for epoxy resin used in the 3D printing at 1 and 3 wt% loadings. Rheological results revealed that the addition of 1 wt% biochar increased the storage modulus, flexural modulus, and strength to 27.5 %, 55.6 %, and 43.3 %, respectively, whereas, viscoelastic and flexural properties of the 3D printed samples decreased with the increased loading of the catalyst.

Based on the high electrical conductivity of BCMs, Bilge et al. (Bilge et al., 2021) prepared a high electrical conductivity microalgae-derived carbon-based material via hydrothermal carbonization and CNTs catalysis. The results indicated that the inoculation of mouse myogenic cells on this scaffold and the application of electrical stimulation were effective in enhancing myotube formation. Furthermore, Ertan et al. (Ertane et al., 2018) reported a wheat stem-derived biochar-based reinforced poly (lactic acid) filament for 3D printing, where the addition of biochar caused stiffness and embrittlement in the product. In addition to the 3D printing, this material was able to be recycled as an agricultural soil amendment owing to its degradable properties.

Microfluidics is a technology based on micrometer channels and small amounts of fluids which has displayed great advantages in various disciplines and fields (Whitesides, 2006). The inherent properties of BCMs can be fully exploited due to the larger interface between the material and the fluid in the microfluidic systems. Microfluidic fuel cells have the advantages of being cheaper, smaller, and without physical membranes compared to conventional fuel cells. Kumar et al. (Kumar et al., 2019) reported the use of biomass-derived flexible 3D carbon aerogels with carbon shell-enclosed binary metal nanoparticles in CNTs. And the material was used as an electrocatalyst for microfluidic fuel cells. This high-performance catalyst provided a maximum power density and durability of 62.8 mW･cm−2 and 60 h, respectively. In particular, microfluidic strategies are promising platforms for the development of microns and nanomaterials with well-defined physicochemical properties (Hou et al., 2017).

Microfluidics is sometimes used to produce tunable pore size in the microspheres with a sphere size coefficient of variation (CV) typically below to 10 % (Ju et al., 2014). Jin- et al. (Jin et al., 2020) synthesized a biomass-derived microporous carbon sphere (CS) by a microfluidic strategy that exhibited an excellent BET area of 576.1 m2•g−1 and micropore volume of 0.22 cm3•g−1. With the addition of surfactant to the CS used for the CO2 capture, the dynamic adsorption capacity of CO2 and the adsorption rate observed were 1.20 mmol•g−1 and 0.024 min−1, respectively. Recently, 3D printing as an advanced manufacturing technique to fabricate microfluidic systems has attracted attention worldwide. Guo et al. (Guo et al., 2020) combined fluorescent CDs with turtle shells as precursors and colloidal photonic crystals (CPCs) to fabricate CPC/CD bead codes and CPC/CD patterned films with fluorescence properties and structural color, respectively, with the help of microfluidics and 3D microfluidic printing technique. These nanocomposites can be used for multi-signal optoelectronic devices, coding, and anti-counterfeiting.

Societal demands drive the need for precision and complexity in the material, due to which various advanced-level manufacturing techniques are emerging with the growing in need for living. Electrospinning is a powerful and feasible technique for the production of microfibers at the micrometer and nanometer levels (Xue et al., 2019). With the continuous development in the field of electrospinning technology, the incorporation of new strategies and materials has injected new vitality into this technology, which has been widely used as catalytic supports, filtration membranes, energy devices, and biomedical scaffolds. Li et al. (Li et al., 2021a) used a combination of electrospinning and electrospray for the preparation of corn stover-derived biochar-containing precursor membranes and convert them into carbon nanofiber felt. Electrochemical results showed that this carbon nanofiber felt electrode has a capacitance value of 204.4F•g−1 at 0.5 A•g−1, with long-term electrochemical stability and excellent multiplicative performance, making it a favorable electrode material for supercapacitors.

Atomic layer deposition (ALD) has emerged as an attractive technique for the fabrication of precise and controllable thin films, enabling precise control in the thickness with the control in the number of layers (George, 2010). This technique allows the coating of the controlled layers on a variety of complex structures according to the desired properties and it has attracted great research interest in catalysis, microelectronics, energy storage, desalination, and medicine. Wang and Su (Wang & Su, 2022) deposited ultrathin Al2O3 layers with variation in the thicknesses on lotus-derived hard carbon by the ALD technique and used the material as the anodes of NIBs. The initial irreversible capacity loss was reduced from 108 to 97 mAh•g−1 and the Coulomb efficiency was increased from 67 % to 72 % compared to the electrode without the Al2O3 coating. The introduction of the control and uniform manner of coating protects the electrodes by reducing the formation of interphase layers of the solid electrolyte, which ultimately improves the electrochemical catalytic activity. The inherent low cost, easy availability, environmental friendliness, and catalytic performance are the major advantages of the BCMs which make them equally potential and promising materials in the other advanced manufacturing technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trends in biowaste upcycling and multi-system coupling for energy and environmental management
ce:section.ce:para: Every year nature provides about 170 billion tons of biomass each year, which creates an abundant source of biomass for human activities. These biomass are the only replenishable carbon source available on the earth that can be converted into liquid, biochar, and gaseous form of hydrocarbon (Yang et al., 2018). However, the biomass that can be used by human being accounts for only 3.5 wt%, and nearly 1.3 billion tons of biowaste are abandoned each year (Bhatia et al., 2018b; Roper, 2002). Therefore, the government and other management departments have spent high energetic and monetary costs on the disposal and management of the biowaste (Cheng et al., 2020a). Studies have shown that the leachate produced by unreasonable landfill of biowaste contains organic and nitrogen pollutants, which pollute surface water and groundwater, and may lead to uncontrolled production of methane thereby increasing the concentration of greenhouse gas on our planet (Madeddu et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2018b).

According to RedCorn et al. (RedCorn et al., 2018), in the US, food waste alone contributes to 3.4 × 107 tons of equivalent emission of carbon dioxide. In addition, the leakage of harmful substances in waste will lead to the destruction of natural resources such as water and soil, and will cause serious problems for environmental health (Watson et al., 2018b). In addition to landfills, a considerable number of solid wastes are treated with the combustion technique. Firstly, waste incineration pollutes the atmospheric environment (Watson et al., 2018b), and secondly, the combustion of the biowaste results in the production of toxic compounds such as dioxins. With the popularization of the concept of ‘low-carbon life and low-carbon economy’, the establishment of a biowaste supervision system and biowaste upcycling in the form of recycling technology has gained practical significance. Environmental and economic benefits of using biowaste in the advanced food manufacturing processes have been presented in Fig. 8
( Weber et al., 2023 ).

The EU’s research on biowaste has been at the forefront of the entire world (Baiano, 2014). Considering the different characteristics of biowaste and combining it with the different recycling processes, Madeddu et al. (Madeddu et al., 2021) used the “Biocascade Approach” to maximize the use of biowaste to ensure the maximum usage and development of the biomass (Madeddu et al., 2021). Hence, we must understand that biomass is a misplaced asset that offers promising options for the sustainable synthesis of fuel and other value-added chemicals, rather than just dispose the biowaste for nothing.

Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2020c) prepared hierarchical porous carbons via the combination of alkali activation and catalytic carbonization, resulting in a high carbon yield of 42.5 wt%. The carbon material has shown ultrahigh specific surface area of 3549 m2•g−1, large meso-/macropores volume of 1.64 cm3•g−1, and high capacitance of 440 Fg−1 under KOH aqueous electrolyte conditions, providing an economical and effective strategy for the application of biowaste materials in high-performance energy storage. Agricultural waste such as straw, shell, and wood can be burned at high temperatures in anaerobic combustion. Biochar can not only be used as soil amendments to improve the fertility and environment of the soil, but also can be applied to the adsorption of pollutants in wastewater and the degradation of toxic substances present in the atmosphere (Weber & Quicker, 2018).

In addition, energy can be released through biochemical processes, usually in the presence of microorganisms or enzymes to convert hemicellulose and cellulose into sugars and produce ethanol with the fermentation process (Cheng et al., 2020a). However, compared with the thermochemical process, the biochemical method is more stringent in the selection of raw materials from the biowaste, the lignocellulose biomass cannot be directly treated, and the reaction process is time-consuming. Secondly, bioenergy is a carbon–neutral fuel. To cope with greenhouse gas emissions, there is an urgent need to control the dependence and consumption of fossil energy for sustainable development. Besides, bioenergy has become a promising choice to promote the replacement of fossil fuels.

Catalytic upgradation of the waste biomass to value-added fuel is a key step towards the circular economy (Varghese, 2019). Second-generation biofuels produced from non-edible biowaste are promising because of their carbon neutrality and sustainability, while avoiding space and water competition with agricultural food products which is a limitation of the first-generation biofuels (Cheng et al., 2020a). The third and fourth-generation biofuels synthesized by the algae biomass are limited by the technology due to the lack of sufficient raw materials, high production costs, and environmental and health-related risks (Cheng et al., 2020a). However, with continuous development and evaluation in the future, the development of biofuels will still have great potential and be feasible for use in aviation fuel (Cheng et al., 2020a).

The study also found that incorporating biowaste into power generation has the advantages of low cost, low risk, convenient implementation, and high efficiency. In the past few decades, biomass-derived materials have attracted high attention as ideal candidate materials for energy production, energy conversion, energy storage, and supercapacitors due to their being renewable, eco-friendly, and available in environmental resources (Yu et al., 2019). Due to the dielectric and conductive properties of biomass-derived materials, these materials have good microwave properties and have become lightweight, low-cost, and green microwave absorbers (Xu et al., 2019b; Zhao et al., 2019). Peymanfar et al. (Peymanfar et al., 2021) used “Populus euphratica harvest” as a new raw material to prepare a pure and uniform CMT by the pyrolysis method, and designed an immobilized carrollite (Cu(Co, Ni)2S4) as a new spinel sulfide microwave absorber on the surface of FCMT, which was functionalized by innovative complementary methods. The antibacterial properties of the prepared nano-conforming material and the use of PAN as an absorption medium have improved mechanical properties compared to traditional waxes and have developed practical applications for customized conforming materials.

Producing hydrogen (H2) from the biowaste is another innovative way to solve both waste as well as energy crises, i.e., using photocatalysts to absorb energy from the sun and decompose wastewater into organic molecules and hydrogen gas. Photo-reforming-based hydrogen production technology, while producing clean H2 and other chemicals, it results in the reduction of the production of waste and thus, achieves the goal of a sustainable chemical industry (Uekert et al., 2020). In addition, photo-reforming can be combined with the reduction of CO2 into CO or other possible fuels, nitrogen fixation in ammonia, and even the reduction of metals present in wastewater or electronic waste. It can utilize the energy and chemical components contained in most of the biowaste and promote the development of a low-carbon circular economy from hydrogen production, waste conversion into fuel, and waste management (Uekert et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prospects of constructing efficiently coupled multi-systems for biowaste upcycling based on AI
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, researchers have made the biowaste management industry sustainable and profitable through advanced artificial intelligence systems. Since most waste management problems are complex and ill-defined, the lack of appropriate waste management practices undermines environmental health and even human health. At present, in dealing with waste management, the application of traditional mechanism models and strict algorithms cannot provide a good enough solution, while artificial intelligence technology can be designed by using computer systems and programs that imitate human characteristics. Through a series of artificial intelligence models, an integrated system based on the intelligent Internet of Things is designed, which can solve unknown problems, predict the results, and configure complex mapping. It provides an alternative and effective method for the application of AI technology in the field of waste management (Sohag & Podder, 2020).

At present, artificial intelligence has been widely used in various fields of waste management. It can solve problems such as water pollution, air pollution, wastewater treatment models, soil remediation and groundwater pollution simulation, and solid waste strategic planning. Since traditional methods with mechanistic models and rigorous algorithms are in many cases insufficient to solve complex problems, especially those which involve the lack of data. The artificial intelligence model can serve to develop the waste generation modes, optimize the waste collection vehicle routes, locate the waste management facilities, simulate the waste conversion processes, and effectively manage the waste.

In the field of waste recycling, artificial intelligence helps to develop better recycling routes in waste management through algorithms, reduces in the overall cost of transportation, and reduces the overall wastage in handling the resources. Novakowski and co-workers (Nowakowski et al., 2020) have applied Harmony Search (HS) algorithms to optimize the route for the waste vehicle in the e-waste collection support system. The optimization results are better than the literature reported in the other artificial intelligence algorithms, and the number of visited collection points increased from 1.2 % to 6.6 %, depending on the comparison of the algorithm (Nowakowski et al., 2020). Based on deep learning algorithms utilized in computer vision, which is used to identify the components of urban solid waste and the construction of the compositions of the biowaste (Dong et al., 2022b). But AI-based solid waste management (SWM) systems remain in the exploration phase. In artificial intelligence, determining the limitations of these technologies is essential for the future planning of robust SWM applications (Abdallah et al., 2020).

Artificial intelligence also plays an important role in the process of biowaste catalytic upgradation to the catalysts (Varghese, 2019). The electronic design of biowaste upgrading catalysts can be achieved by combining multi-scale computing technology with machine learning. Researchers have used molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, density functional theory (DFT), and microdynamics modeling to analyze the catalytic reaction process and its mechanism. The DFT calculations and statistical methods have also been applied to explore the identification of catalytic descriptors and the creation of scaling relationships (Varghese, 2019). Taking porous carbon as an example, solid waste-derived porous carbon (SWDPC) captures carbon dioxide while mitigating climate change and improving solid waste management, and it is an effective tool for reducing carbon dioxide emissions (Yuan et al., 2022).

Artificial intelligence is guided by machine learning and develops from the aspects of synthetic route, CO2 capture ability, process cycle ability, and sample optimization for the innovation in the carbon capture methods and improve the overall environmental benefits of the carbon dioxide capture methods. Yuan et al. (Yuan et al., 2022) designed the most predictive Gradient Boosting Decision Tree (GBDT) by mapping the adsorption of CO2 in the assembly data set system as the structural, composition characteristics, and adsorption parameter function of biowaste-derived porous carbon, effectively guiding the synthesis of porous carbon for CO2 adsorption applications (Yuan et al., 2021).

Biowaste conversion can achieve carbon neutrality and environmental sustainability. Unique 2D structures can be designed from biowaste with fascinating properties. Fabrication and formation mechanisms of BCMs were reviewed in plasma hybrid system. A better understanding of the mechanism for the preparation of BCMs from a low-temperature plasma hybrid system can contribute to the manipulation in the fabrication technique as well as in the application for maximum impact. Plasma modified BCM has a new transition lattice phase and exhibits high resilience. The mechanism behind the formation of BCMs produced by the plasma technique needs to better control the various experimental conditions required in the plasma process. Artificial intelligence is beneficial for applications in biowaste management, which is beneficial for better control in the synthesis and applications of BCMs. The various conditions applied in the plasma treatment directly affect the size, shape, and properties of BCMs in a significant manner, therefore, enabling the controllable synthesis. The upcycling of biowaste into advanced carbon materials has a positive consequence on mitigating climate change and creating a community with a greener future.

Fanghua Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Gaotingyue Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Bachirou Guene Lougou: Writing – review & editing. Qiaoqiao Zhou: Writing – review & editing. Boshu Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Yong Shuai: Writing – review & editing.
